beats.text: Translation pairs sequences of different lengths and languages with no word-by-word alignment, which a plain recurrent net cannot handle.
Sequence-to-sequence uses two recurrent nets: one encoder reads the source, one decoder writes the translation.
The encoder consumes source tokens one at a time and its final hidden state becomes a single context vector summarizing the sentence.

visual.note: final encoder state as the whole-sentence summary

beats.text: The decoder factorizes the output autoregressively, conditioning each token on all prior outputs and on the same frozen context vector.

visual.note: every output token sees the same c

beats.text: Two LSTMs trained end-to-end on millions of pairs reached competitive WMT 14 English to French BLEU, rivaling phrase-based systems.
The same framework generalizes to summarization, dialogue, code generation, speech-to-text, and image captioning with different encoders.

visual.label: encoder layers in the original deep LSTM

beats.text: Every clause, entity, and nuance of a long source sentence must be squeezed into one fixed-dimensional vector and held unchanged.

visual.sub: one vector for the whole sentence

beats.text: The encoder's last state is a lossy, length-blind summary, so it must remember a forty-word sentence in a constant number of bits.

visual.note: width never grows with input length

beats.text: BLEU is fine on short sentences but falls off a cliff as length grows, a decay Cho and colleagues documented directly in 2014.
This is an information-theoretic ceiling, not a tuning problem; you cannot store an arbitrarily long sentence without loss.

visual.label: words where fixed-vector quality has collapsed

beats.text: The intuition is writing a translation from memory without looking back at the page; attention lets the decoder glance back.
Bahdanau, Cho and Bengio kept all encoder hidden states, one annotation per source word, instead of collapsing to one vector.

visual.sub: keep one annotation per source word

beats.text: An alignment model, a tiny feedforward net inside a tanh, scores how well the decoder state matches each source annotation.

visual.note: additive score, learned jointly with the translator

beats.text: Softmax over source positions turns raw scores into attention weights, and the context vector is their weighted sum of annotations.

visual.note: a soft, differentiable lookup into the encoder

beats.text: Worked over four words with scores one, zero, half, negative half, the softmax gives weights that sum to exactly one.
The context now varies per output step; translating the verb pulls weight onto the source verb, and the frozen vector is gone.
This is the original attention mechanism, three years before Attention Is All You Need, with alignment never supervised.

visual.label: attention weights always sum to one

beats.text: Luong, Pham and Manning simplified the idea: if query and key share a space, a plain dot product already measures alignment.

visual.sub: a dot product instead of an MLP

beats.text: They defined three scoring functions of increasing flexibility: dot, general with one learned matrix, and concat which recovers Bahdanau.

visual.tex: \mathrm{score}(s_i,h_j) = \begin{cases} s_i^{\top} h_j & \text{dot} \\ s_i^{\top} W_a h_j & \text{general} \\ v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a[s_i;h_j]) & \text{concat} \end{cases}

beats.text: A dot product is a single GPU-friendly matrix multiply, faster and more memory-efficient than the additive small network.
At large dimension the dot product needs the famous one-over-square-root-of-d-k rescaling to keep softmax out of saturation.

visual.note: the variance fix the Transformer would adopt

beats.text: Luong used the current decoder state rather than the previous one, and split attention into global versus local windows.
Luong's general form, with projections renamed W-Q and W-K, is exactly scaled dot-product attention, the Transformer's descendant.
By 2016 attention rode on top of recurrence, and that sequentiality capped GPU parallelism and gradient reach across long sentences.
Self-attention points the same mechanism at a sequence's own positions, replacing recurrence so every token mixes in one parallel step.
Scaled dot-product becomes the core operation, and running several heads in parallel subspaces gives multi-head attention.
Dropping recurrence loses all sense of order, so position is injected directly into the embeddings as positional encodings.
The same skeleton survives: score every key, softmax, weighted sum of values, with the MLP scorer swapped for a cheap dot product.

visual.tex: e_{ij} = v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a s_i + U_a h_j) \;\longrightarrow\; e_{ij} = \frac{(W_Q s_i)^{\top}(W_K h_j)}{\sqrt{d_k}}

beats.text: The 2014 bottleneck and the 2017 Transformer are two ends of one short straight line, and everything since is this idea scaled up.

visual.label: year recurrence was dropped for pure attention
